title: Testosterone to Estradiol Ratio Calculator
tagline: Calculate the testosterone-to-estradiol ratio for hormone balance assessment in hormonal health, fertility, and endocrine wellness.
intro: Enter testosterone and estradiol levels with their units to compute the T:E2 ratio and see an interpretation against reference ranges.

male.low: Low ratio — may indicate relative oestrogen excess (possible aromatase activity, obesity, or liver disease)
male.high: High ratio — relative oestrogen deficiency (consult an endocrinologist)

female.low: Low ratio — may reflect high oestrogen or low testosterone
female.high: Elevated ratio for females — may reflect low oestrogen or elevated androgens

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive numbers for testosterone and estradiol levels.
error.estradiol: Estradiol must be greater than zero.

about.heading: About the testosterone to estradiol ratio calculator
about.body:
Testosterone and estradiol (oestradiol, E2) are steroid hormones that play complementary roles in both male and female physiology. Testosterone is the primary androgen, driving male sexual development, muscle mass, bone density, libido, and red blood cell production, while also being present in women at lower concentrations where it supports libido, energy, and bone health. Estradiol is the primary oestrogen, essential for female reproductive function, bone mineralisation, cardiovascular health, and mood regulation, and also present in men at levels critical for bone density, sexual function, and lipid metabolism.

The testosterone-to-estradiol (T:E2) ratio is a dimensionless number that reflects the balance between these two hormones. In clinical endocrinology, this ratio provides more diagnostic information than either hormone alone because it captures the relative dominance of androgens versus oestrogens in the body. A low ratio in men may suggest increased aromatase enzyme activity — the enzyme that converts testosterone to oestradiol — which is commonly associated with obesity, alcoholic liver disease, ageing, or use of certain medications. A very high ratio may reflect low oestradiol, which can impair bone health and sexual function even when testosterone is within range.

In women, the T:E2 ratio is more complex because both hormones fluctuate considerably through the menstrual cycle and across the lifespan. During the follicular phase, oestradiol rises significantly while testosterone remains relatively stable, lowering the ratio. Around ovulation, oestradiol peaks and then falls, while testosterone shows a smaller mid-cycle surge. In postmenopausal women, both hormones decline but oestradiol falls more sharply, potentially raising the relative androgen ratio, which may contribute to symptoms such as changes in body composition and skin texture.

The standard way to calculate the T:E2 ratio when testosterone is measured in ng/dL and oestradiol in pg/mL is simply to divide the testosterone value by the oestradiol value. If testosterone is measured in nmol/L, convert to ng/dL by multiplying by 28.84 before dividing. Some laboratories report a different convention; always confirm with your laboratory reference ranges.

This tool is intended for educational and monitoring purposes. It does not replace clinical interpretation by an endocrinologist or reproductive medicine specialist. Hormone levels vary by laboratory assay, time of day, recent diet, exercise, and sleep, so a single value is rarely sufficient for diagnosis without clinical context and repeat testing.

examples.heading: T:E2 ratio examples
examples.intro: Three representative scenarios showing how to interpret the testosterone-to-estradiol ratio.

items.note: Well within the typical male reference range of 10–40. No hormonal imbalance suggested.

items.input: Male with suspected aromatase excess: Testosterone 420 ng/dL, Estradiol 58 pg/mL
items.note: Low ratio suggests relative oestrogen excess. May warrant investigation for obesity, liver disease, or aromatase inhibitor consideration.
Low ratio expected during mid-follicular phase when oestradiol is rising. Consistent with normal female cyclical variation.

items.input: Postmenopausal female: Testosterone 30 ng/dL, Estradiol 12 pg/mL
items.note: Relatively elevated ratio post-menopause as oestradiol declines more than testosterone with age.

howto.heading: How to use the testosterone to estradiol ratio calculator
steps: Enter your testosterone level and select whether it is measured in ng/dL or nmol/L; the calculator converts nmol/L to ng/dL automatically before computing the ratio.
Enter your estradiol level in pg/mL (pmol/L values can be converted by dividing by 3.67).
Select your sex and optionally your age and, for females, the phase of the menstrual cycle when the sample was collected.
Click Calculate to see the T:E2 ratio and an interpretation.
Discuss the results with your healthcare provider — single values should always be interpreted alongside symptoms, other labs, and clinical context.

items.q: What is a normal testosterone to estradiol ratio for men?
items.a: Most endocrinologists consider a T:E2 ratio of roughly 10–40 (when testosterone is in ng/dL and oestradiol in pg/mL) to be within the normal range for adult men. However, reference ranges vary between laboratories and between age groups, so always compare your result with the specific ranges from your laboratory report.

items.q: Why would a man have a low T:E2 ratio?
items.a: A low ratio in men often reflects increased aromatase activity, the enzyme that converts testosterone to estradiol. Common causes include obesity (adipose tissue is rich in aromatase), alcoholic or cirrhotic liver disease, ageing, use of opioids or certain antifungal medications, and rare conditions such as testicular tumours producing oestrogen. In some cases low testosterone with normal oestradiol also produces a low ratio.

items.q: Does the T:E2 ratio matter for bone health?
items.a: Yes, particularly in men. Oestradiol plays a crucial role in bone mineralisation in both sexes. Men with very low oestradiol levels — even if testosterone is normal — tend to have lower bone mineral density and higher fracture risk. A high T:E2 ratio (indicating low oestradiol) in men therefore warrants evaluation for osteoporosis.

items.q: How does the T:E2 ratio change after menopause?
items.a: After menopause, ovarian oestradiol production ceases abruptly while adrenal androgen production declines more gradually. This typically raises the relative T:E2 ratio in postmenopausal women. The shift may contribute to symptoms such as changes in body fat distribution, skin thickness, and libido that many women experience after menopause.

items.q: How accurate is a single T:E2 measurement?
items.a: Sex hormone levels vary substantially with time of day, recent exercise, dietary fat intake, and stress. For men, morning samples are generally recommended when testosterone is highest. For women, the day of the menstrual cycle profoundly affects both values. A single measurement provides a snapshot; clinical decisions usually require at least two measurements taken under standardised conditions.

items.q: Can this calculator be used to guide hormone replacement therapy?
items.a: The calculator can help you monitor your T:E2 ratio over time, but all decisions about hormone replacement therapy (HRT or TRT) should be made in collaboration with a qualified endocrinologist or hormone specialist. Dosing, formulation, and monitoring protocols require individualised assessment that goes well beyond the ratio alone.
